description: A long implement with a flattened blade is 'rewa' when it moves through water to propel or steer a boat. The word covers an oar working against a rest and a paddle held freely in the hands unless that construction difference matters to the account. 'phora' (sail) receives wind instead of direct strokes.
articulatory_notes: Tap the tongue tip once at the alveolar ridge for 'r' and open into stressed mid-front 'e'. Round the lips while raising the back of the tongue for glide 'w', then lower the jaw into open 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The brief tap, open vowel, and rounded glide give 'rewa' one smooth change of direction that can recall a stroke entering and leaving water.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: An oar turns repeated bodily effort into quiet movement and gives the traveler immediate knowledge of current and depth.

semantic_domains.activity: an implement used in the repeated work of rowing or paddling
semantic_domains.physical: a long water-moving tool with a flattened blade
